records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 190 
highly structured, state-sponsored genealogical systems in the Indian 
subcontinent. Established in 1326 or 1327 AD under the patronage of King 
Harisimhadeva of the Karnat dynasty, this extensive archival system was 
designed to regulate marriage alliances, maintain caste endogamy, and 
prevent consanguineous marriages among the Maithil Brahmins and Karna 
Kayasthas. The system divided the community into ranked hierarchical 
categories, primarily Shrotriya (or Soit), Yogya (or Jog), and Panjibaddh, 
with unregistered families categorized as Jaibar. 
The origin of the Panji Prabandh is traditionally attributed to a socio-legal 
crisis involving Pandit Harinath Upadhyaya, a renowned court scholar of 
Dharmashastra in the village of Satadhara. Due to the absence of systematic, 
written genealogical records, Pandit Harinath Upadhyaya unwittingly 
married a woman who was a maternal cousin within the prohibited degrees 
of consanguinity (Sapinda). The marital transgression came to light after his 
wife was accosted by a man of the lower-caste Dusadh community. Though a 
black snake miraculously intervened to save her, rumors of an improper 
meeting forced her to undergo an ordeal by fire, holding a red-hot iron rod to 
prove her chastity. When her hands burned, she was declared guilty under 
standard interpretations, yet she was determined to prove her purity. 
She approached Lakhima Thakurain (also known as Lakkhima Devi), a 
highly learned female philosopher and the wife of the famous lawgiver 
Chandeshvara Thakkura. Recognizing that the wife's hands had burned not 
due to unchastity but because of the spiritual pollution of an unrecognized 
Sapinda marriage, Lakhima advised her to request a retrial with a modified 
oath. Upon declaring before the court that she had not had relations with any 
outcaste other than her husband, her hands remained unburned. This 
revelation shocked the community: the court pundit Harinath Upadhyaya had 
committed a serious breach of exogamy by marrying his cousin's daughter’s 
daughter within the fifth degree of maternal kinship, rendering his wife a 
Chandalini in the eyes of the law. 
To prevent such unrecognized transgressions, King Harisimhadeva 
established a state-backed bureaucracy of genealogists known as Panjikars. 
The primary function of these genealogists was to collect family histories and 
issue a Siddhanta (a certificate of matrimonial eligibility) before any 
marriage could be solemnized, confirming that the prospective bride and 
groom did not share common ancestors within the prohibited degrees of 
Sapinda.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 191 
Structural Erasure: The Relational Node and Kinship Syntax 
Within this highly structured patrilineal framework, women were historically 
documented not as independent entities, but strictly as relational nodes—
conduits through which lineages intersected and status was transferred. In the 
standard public registers of the Mool Panji (the root registers) and the Shakha 
Panji (branch books), the personal names of women were systematically 
omitted. This was not a consequence of archival negligence, but a deliberate 
feature of the Panji methodology. Because the system was designed as a 
mathematical apparatus to compute consanguinity (Utedh), the personal 
identity of a female was irrelevant to the calculation. The system required 
tracking her Mool (village of paternal origin), her Gotra (paternal clan), her 
Pravara (sages associated with the clan), and her father's paternal pedigree. 
Consequently, women were recorded solely through a standardized kinship 
syntax that defined them in relation to their closest male relatives. A typical 
entry in the palm-leaf manuscripts (patras) documented a female through 
specific Sanskrit and Maithili kinship terms and abbreviations. 
Notation / Kinship Term | Sanskrit / Maithili Script | Meaning | 
Genealogical Context & Application 
Suta / Putri | sutā / putrī | Daughter of | Links a female node directly to her 
father's lineage to trace her paternal descent line.4 
Patni / Vallabha | patnī / vallabhā | Wife of | Connects a female node to 
her husband's family line, marking the formal union of two lineages.4 
Dau (or Ddau) | dau / ddau | Daughter's son (Dauhita) / Maternal 
Grandfather | Indicates the maternal grandfather (Matamaha) or mother's 
maternal grandfather (Matrmatamaha) to verify the maternal side of the 
pedigree.4 
Adya | ādyā | First / Primary wife | Designates the first wife or the primary 
matrimonial alliance of a male, used to establish the primary line of descent.4 
Apara | aparā | Subsequent wife / Subsequent marriage | Documents 
second, third, or subsequent wives, essential for tracking complex 
hypergamous and polygamous families.4 
Săñ | sa~ | From / Originating from | Identifies the ancestral village 
(Mool-gram) associated with the family branch.4 
This syntax ensured that the maternal grandfather's lineage (Matrika) could 
be systematically computed. A woman's pedigree was expressed as a 
relational chain, such as:

records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 192 
By converting female identities into functional relational nodes, the Panji 
system maintained mathematical precision while systematically erasing the 
personal names of women from the standard public record. 
The Cross-Referencing Coordinates and Lineage Algebra 
To manage the enormous volume of data across generations without 
duplicating long paternal lineages, Panjikars developed a highly sophisticated 
indexing and cross-referencing coordinate system. Instead of transcribing a 
maternal grandfather's entire paternal tree each time his daughter's children 
were recorded, the Panjikar used a coordinate code consisting of the page 
number and line number of the grandfather's primary entry in the Shakha 
Panji. 
For example, an entry in the Shakha Panji might contain a coordinate note 
such as "114/4". This coordinate directed the reader to Folio 114, Line 4 of 
the manuscript, where the full paternal pedigree of Rav Shankar of the 
Valiyasa mool was documented. His marriage to the daughter of Pratitnath of 
the Pavouli mool was fully mapped at coordinate "103/2". By navigating 
these coordinates, a genealogist could instantly reconstruct the maternal 
lineage (Matrik) of any individual across several centuries, maintaining a 
compact and manageable database on fragile palm leaves. 
This algebraic tracking of maternal lineages was crucial for computing the 
sixteen consanguineous nodes, known as the Chhatis. Before a marriage 
could be approved, the Panjikar calculated these sixteen generational 
boundaries for the prospective bride to ensure she did not overlap with the 
groom's prohibited maternal or paternal lines. 
Chhati Node | Relationship Definition in the Panji Syntax | Prohibited 
Degree of Kinship 
First Chhati | Paternal Great-Grandfather's Paternal Grandfather | 6th 
generation from paternal line.4 
Second Chhati | Paternal Great-Grandfather's Maternal Grandfather | 6th 
generation from maternal line.4 
Third Chhati | Paternal Grandfather's Maternal Grandfather's Father | 6th 
generation from maternal line.4 
Fourth Chhati | Paternal Grandfather's Maternal Grandfather's Father-in-law 
| 6th generation from maternal line.4 
Fifth Chhati | Paternal Grandmother's Paternal Great-Grandfather | 6th 
generation from paternal line.4

If any of these sixteen Chhati lines of the bride overlapped with the groom’s 
paternal line within seven generations, or maternal line within five 
generations, the marriage was declared Anadhikar (lacking rights) and was 
forbidden. 
Contextual Exceptions: The Socioeconomics of theRasarh-Araria Sub-
Archive 
The absolute omission of female personal names from Maithil genealogies 
contains one notable historical exception. Panji manuscripts and primary 
Maithili studies explicitly record that: 
*"Only in theRasarh-Araria Panji is the mention of women's/female names 
found 
in 
the 
Panji." 
("mātrarasār̤ ha-
arariyākapañjīmestrīgaṇakakanāmakaullekhapañjīmebheṭaitaachi"* ) 4 
This unique recording of female personal names within the registers of the 
Rasarh-Araria branch—located on the eastern frontier of Mithila, spanning

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 194 
the Purnea and Araria districts of modern-day Bihar—represents a significant 
archival deviation. A social and economic analysis of this region explains 
why personal names became necessary in this specific geographic sub-
archive. 
Historically, the eastern districts of Mithila were dominated by large, wealthy 
estate holdings, notably held by the Srinagar and Champanagar branches of 
royal landholders (Zamindars). Under British colonial land policies, 
particularly the Permanent Settlement of 1793, the exact legal identification 
of individuals holding land or receiving maintenance allowances became a 
matter of strict legal and civil litigation. 
The ruling families of these eastern estates frequently practiced the transfer 
of large, rent-free landed properties—referred to as Devotter Mahals and 
Talukas—directly to their daughters, sisters, and widows for their lifelong 
maintenance. For example, when brothers in these noble families divided 
their estates, large tracts of land, such as two hundred bighas of rent-free land 
at Mouja-Rasarh, were formally settled in the names of female descendants 
for their maintenance. 
The legal and administrative records of the Srinagar branch preserve the 
specific names of these women, including Yog Maya Dai (assigned the Bara 
Singhoul Taluka), Maha Maya Dai (assigned the Kedali Taluka), Nity Maya 
Dai (assigned the Chakla Taluka), Maina Dai (assigned the Shahpur Taluka), 
Baba Dai, Kirtimaya, and Dharmamaya Daiji. Under the jurisdiction of 
British courts and traditional land registries, recording a female merely as 
"the daughter of Abhaynath" or "the granddaughter of Hari" was legally 
insufficient to protect her land tenure or to secure her monthly maintenance 
allowances. 
To prevent estate disputes and satisfy the requirements of colonial land 
registries, the local Panjikars of the Rasarh-Araria branch adapted their 
traditional methods. They began recording the actual, personal names of 
these female landholders directly in the Panji registers. This synthesis of 
traditional ritual genealogy with colonial property rights forced the Panjikars 
of this sub-region to depart from the structural anonymity that characterized 
the rest of Mithila. 
Transgressive Inscriptions: Female Names in theDooshanPanji 
The structural documentation of women also played a critical role in the 
Dooshan Panji (popularly termed "The Black Book" or the register of

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 195 
pollutions). While the public *ShakhaPanjis projected an unblemished 
narrative of caste purity and hypergamous elevation, the secret Dooshan 
Panji* served as a pragmatist's counter-archive, detailing lineage 
"pollutions," illicit alliances, marriages within prohibited degrees, and inter-
caste unions. 
In the Dooshan Panji, the documentation of women was highly precise, 
recording alliances with women from outside the accepted endogamous 
boundaries. This secret record was maintained because, under traditional 
rules, a family that had suffered a "pollution" could gradually regain its status 
and achieve "purification" (Purnatah Shuddha) after five or six generations of 
strictly verified endogamous marriages. 
A prominent historical example documented within the Dooshan Panji 
concerns the family of Gangesh Upadhyaya, the 14th-century founder of the 
Navya-Nyaya (New Logic) school of Indian philosophy. Publicly, Gangesh 
Upadhyaya was celebrated as a Jagadguru and Param Guru. However, the 
Dooshan Panji preserves the private reality: 
*"From theChhadanroot, the wife (Vallabha) of Gangesh, the author 
ofTattvachintamani, wasCharmakarini[belonging to the tannercaste]...It is 
recorded that Gangesh was born five years after the passing of his father 
(PitriParokshePanch VarshVyatite)."* 4 
This entry reveals that Gangesh was born five years after his father's death, 
and his mother is recorded as a lower-caste woman named Medha. 
Additionally, the Dooshan Panji preserves the rare, explicit names of women 
in cases of extreme genealogical anomalies. In the Devanand Panji, the 
daughter of Gangesh’s son Supan is explicitly named "Gora": 
*"JagadguruGaṅgeśsutSupandauBhaṇḍārisam-sañHarādityadau. 
Putrasutā-chaGorāJajivāl-sañJīvepatnī..."* 4 
Furthermore, the Dooshan Panji provides a detailed, generation-by-
generation mapping of how lower-caste maternal lines were tracked across 
centuries until their descendants achieved purification.

Through this highly systematic tracking of maternal descent, the Dooshan 
Panji ensured that the genetic and social "pollutions" brought by women 
from outside the caste were monitored over six generations, until the lineage 
could once again be integrated into the public, elite hypergamous marriage 
pool. 
Philosophical and Literary Dimensions of Elite Women 
The systematic erasure of female personal names in standard Maithil 
genealogies stands in stark contrast to the prominent intellectual and political 
roles played by elite women in Mithila's history. While ordinary women were 
strictly bound by the purdah (veiling) system and hypergamous dependency, 
noble and scholarly women frequently managed estates, patronized great 
poets, and made significant contributions to orthodox Hindu philosophy.

These women achieved significant personal renown and wielded substantial 
political and intellectual power. Yet, under the rules of the Panji Prabandh, 
their individual names were excluded from the standard public genealogies. 
This dichotomy demonstrates that the Panji was not a historical narrative 
designed to honor individuals, but a highly specialized, functional database 
built to compute caste purity and exogamous boundaries. 
Conclusion: The Contemporary Trajectory ofMaithilGenealogy 
Over seven centuries, the Panji Prabandh served as the social and genetic 
backbone of the Mithila region, preserving an unbroken record of hundreds 
of thousands of family trees. Within this complex system, women were 
historically documented as relational nodes designed to calculate caste purity 
and exogamous boundaries. The unique exception of the Rasarh-Araria Panji 
demonstrates 
how 
traditional 
genealogical 
practices 
adapted 
to 
socioeconomic shifts, recording female personal names to secure legal land 
titles under colonial rule. 
In the modern era, the traditional Panji system faces significant challenges. 
Urbanization, global migration, and the rise of digital matchmaking 
platforms have led many younger families to prioritize education and career 
compatibility over ancestral lineage verification. However, the historic palm-
leaf manuscripts and basaha paper scrolls of the Panjikars remain an 
invaluable archive. Efforts by organizations like the National Mission for 
Manuscripts to digitize these centuries-old records represent a crucial step 
toward preserving Mithila’s unique cultural and sociological heritage.

PRĀCĪN PAÑJĪ OF MITHILA 
Khaṇḍa 1: The Ancient Genealogy 
A Comprehensive Research Report on Villages, Families, Lineages, 
Gotras, Scholarly Titles & Genealogical Structures 
Source: Genome Mapping (450 AD – 2009 AD) — Panji Prabandha of 
Mithila, Khaṇḍa 1 
Authors: Gajendra Thakur · Nagendra Kumar Jha · Panjikar Vidyanand Jha 
Publisher: Shruti Publication, New Delhi, 2012 | ISBN 978-93-80538-62-4 
Analysis prepared for Videha Parallel Literature Movement | May 2026 
1. Overview of Khaṇḍa 1: Prācīn Pañjī 
Khaṇḍa 1 (khaṇḍa 1 – prācīna pañjī, 'The Ancient Genealogy') constitutes the 
foundational stratum of the Panji Prabandha corpus of Mithila. Spanning 
pages 1. through 1. of the digitised volume, it preserves the oldest surviving 
genealogical record of Maithil Brahmin families across the Tirhut heartland, 
rendered in a bilingual format — the left column in Mithilakshara-influenced 
Devanagari, and the right column in Kaithi-transliterated Devanagari — with 
both columns carrying identical genealogical data. This duplication reflects 
the Panjikar's practice of ensuring legibility across two distinct scribal 
traditions. 
The text opens with the invocatory formula 'Atha patra pañjī likhyate' (atha 
patra pañjī likhate / atha sapatra pañjī likhate) — 'Now the genealogical

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 200 
register is written' — establishing its ritual-juridical function as an 
authoritative record, not merely a genealogical chronicle. The corpus is 
structured as a cascade of village-clusters (mūla-grāma) linked by the 
genealogical notation system employing suta (son), sutā (daughter), 
dau/dautā (maternal nephew's son, i.e., the individual being placed), and 
bīji/vīji (maternal line anchor). 
The historical anchor of Khaṇḍa 1 is Halāyudha of Gaṅgolī — identified in 
the opening entry as the founder-patriarch whose descendants branch across 
more than seventy named villages. The text then proceeds through over fifty 
named sections (numbered 1 through 5 in the document, each marking a 
genealogical sub-cycle or mātṛka-cakra) before concluding with a colophon 
recording the scribe and date: Śāka 1778 / Saṃvat 1863, Śrāvaṇa Kṛṣṇa 
Tṛtīyā — approximately August 1856 CE — attesting to the most recent 
redaction of this 'ancient' layer of the Pañjī. 
2. Geographical Coverage: Villages and Settlement Clusters 
2.1 Named Villages (Grāma) — Complete Register 
Khaṇḍa 1 names at least 122 distinct localities. These range from major 
mūla-grāma (home-clusters with Panjikar classification authority) to 
secondary settlements where branches of families are recorded as resident.

markers without formal mūla designation, indicating the corpus covers a 
geographic spread of roughly 150+ distinct inhabited locations across the 
Tirhut administrative division.

Analytical note: The gotra list in section 14 is unusual in including a verse 
for the Kādya (Kāśyapa) gotra: 'dānaśauryaṃ pratāpaiśca prasiddhā yatra 
pārthivāḥ — Oinisā sarvataḥ śreṣṭhā svosva-dharma-pravartikāḥ' — 'Those 
rulers who are renowned for liberality, valour and majesty; the Oinisā are the 
best in all respects, upholding their own dharma.' This verse links the 
Kādya/Kāśyapa gotra explicitly to ksatriya (royal) ancestry, explaining the 
special status of this gotra cluster and its association with the Karnāṭa 
dynasty of Mithilā, particularly Nānyadeva. 
4. Notable Families and Lineage Chains 
4.1 Historical Anchor: Halāyudha of Gaṅgolī 
The opening entry of Khaṇḍa 1 establishes Halāyudha (halāyudha) as the 
genealogical primus. His descendants from Gaṅgolī form the ur-trunk from 
which two sons — Vīra and Nārāyaṇa — branch out. Nārāyaṇa's son 
Śūlapāṇi (śūlapāṇi) then generates two sons, Rāleś and Śāṅkau (possibly 
Śānkara), initiating the register for Khaṇḍa Barā (Khaṇḍabalā) village. This 
opening sequence (pages 1.17–1.) is the oldest generational stratum in the 
corpus. Halāyudha is historically identified as the court scholar (Mahāmātya) 
of Vallāla Sena of Bengal (c. 1175–1225 CE), placing the genealogical origin 
squarely in the late 12th century. 
The notation '(05/04)' following Vīra indicates his genealogical position code 
— 05 = birth order rank among brothers, 04 = total siblings — a data-
encoding technique unique to the Pañjī system, enabling reconstruction of 
family size at each generation. 
4.2 The Mahāmahopādhyāya Scholarly Lineages 
Khaṇḍa 1 records at least 47 holders of the title Mahāmahopādhyāya 
(abbreviated ma.ma.u., ma.ma.upā., or spelled out fully). These represent the 
highest scholarly honour in Mithilā, awarded to masters of the Navya-Nyāya 
tradition and related Brahmanical sciences. The major Mahāmahopādhyāya 
lineages traceable in this volume:

devaśarman | Various | 5th-6th | Known descendant cluster

4.5 Unique Entries and Exceptional Records 
Beyond the standard suta/sutā chains, several entries in Khaṇḍa 1 stand out 
for their anomalous or historically significant character:

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 209 
• 
Dom Tekārī — Brahmin / Non-Brahmin Interface: Page 1. records 'Atha 
Ḍom Ṭekārī grāma tatra Caturvedādhyāyī Kāmadevo bīji' — 'Then the 
village of Ḍom Ṭekārī: therein the four-Veda-studying Kāmadeva is the 
bīji [genealogical anchor].' The designation 'Ḍom Ṭekārī' (a village with 
Ḍom community association) in a Brahmin genealogical register is 
anomalous, suggesting either a Brahmin settlement within or adjacent to 
a Ḍom hamlet, or an early record of caste-boundary ambiguity in Tirhut. 
• 
Ayācī Lineage — The Renunciants: The term 'Ayācī' (ayācī, 'one who 
does not ask/beg') appears as a personal epithet for specific scholars, 
particularly in the Bhavānātha family (page 1.): 'Bhavānātha pra. 
Ayācīsuta Śambhunātha Rudranāthāpaty.' This designates a scholarly 
sub-lineage whose members took a vow of non-solicitation — 
maintaining themselves without requesting gifts — a rare ascetic 
characteristic within householder Brahmin genealogy. 
• 
Tegachchhiya Entries: The term 'Tegachchhiya' (attenuated/thinned 
lineage) appears in several village entries, notably in the Saudarapura 
and Khauāla clusters. These mark lineages where the genealogical chain 
has become so attenuated — through male-line failure, adoption, or out-
migration — that the Panjikar has recorded the family as 'thinned out' 
rather than extinct. This is a formal genealogical status in the Pañjī 
taxonomy. 
• 
Vidyāpati the Poet: Page 1. preserves 'Vidyāpati — Puḍrīka — Machadī' 
— 'Vidyāpati [settled at] Puḍrīka-Machadī [village].' This is a direct 
genealogical placement of the great Maithilī poet Vidyāpati (c. 1352–
1448 CE) within the Pañjī matrix, confirming his Māithil Brahmin status 
and ancestral village identification. 
• 
Mādhavanātha (Mīthaul) — The Calcified Formula: Page 1. records an 
extraordinary chain where the formula 'e sutau' (and his son) repeats 
through 18 generations without interruption — the longest continuous e-
sutau chain in the document. This likely represents an idealised or 
schematised deep-time pedigree rather than verified historical record. 
• 
Female Ancestors Named: At page 1., 'Nona sutā (Nona prabhṛti) 
Rūpamātī — Māṇḍal' appears — a rare instance where a female ancestor 
is named (Rūpamātī, 'beautiful mother') rather than merely designated as 
the unnamed maternal link. The Pañjī normally renders women invisible 
in the patrilineal chain; named women represent exceptions of unusual 
status.

5.2 The Mātṛkā-Cakra (Maternal Cycle) System 
A distinctive structural feature of Khaṇḍa 1 is the mātṛkā-cakra (maternal 
cycle), explicitly invoked with the formula 'evaṃ X mātṛka-cakraṃ' — 'thus 
the maternal cycle of X [is completed].' These cycles mark the completion of 
one complete genealogical circuit through the dau-bīji network, establishing 
that every living male in the register is validly connected to his assigned 
mūla-grāma through both his father's line (genealogy) and his mother's clan 
(mātṛkā). The document records over 55 named mātṛkā-cakra completions 
within Khaṇḍa 1, each effectively certifying the social legitimacy of the 
connected families. 
Notable mātṛkā-cakra completions in Khaṇḍa 1 include: Śrīdhara-mātṛka-
cakra (page 1.), Śore-mātṛka-cakra (page 1.), Gaṅgādhara-mātṛka-cakra 
(page 1.), Baidhyanātha-mātṛka-cakra (page 1.), Manori-mātṛka-cakra, 
Pañcadhaneśa-mātṛka-cakra, and Bachhāī-mātṛka-cakra among others.

7. The Colophon and Scribal Record 
Page 1. preserves a partial colophon embedded within a genealogical entry, 
recording the conditions of the manuscript's production: 
|| śubha śāke 1 san 1 sāla śrāvaṇa kṛṣṇatṛtīyāyāṃ dinakarau pāṇḍuā sa~ pā~ 
rāmānanda likhita midaṃ ṭha. lakṣmīdhara suto gaṇeśa: || 
Translation: 'Auspicious: In Śaka 1778 [= c. 1856 CE], year 1263 [Saṃvat], 
on the third day of the dark fortnight of Śrāvaṇa [approximately July–
August], from Paṇḍuā [village], Pañcā Rāmānanda has written this. [The 
entry concerns:] Ṭhākura Lakṣmīdhara's son Gaṇeśa.' 
This colophon reveals: (1) the scribe's name — Rāmānanda, with honorific 
Pañcā (Pañca = five, possibly a caste sub-designation or five-fold scholar 
title); (2) his home village — Paṇḍuā; (3) the precise date — Śrāvaṇa Kṛṣṇa 
Tṛtīyā, Śaka 1778, which converts to approximately August 1856 CE; (4) 
that this is a scribal copy (likhita), not the original composition. The original 
composition is attributed to the Panjikar tradition itself, stretching back to the 
13th century CE. 
A second scribal note appears in the body of section 77 (page 1.): 'Jhaṃū. 
Na. Śākhā lekhakaḥ Ḍomāī Miśrā / Monaśārvā pustaka / Tacita pañcāri lino 
śoṃśe' — indicating at least two distinct scribal traditions (the Jhāṃū-Nava 
Śākhā and the Monaśārvā tradition) contributed to Khaṇḍa 1's compilation, 
lending the document a composite textual character typical of the larger Pañjī 
corpus.

Gajendra Thakur — 213 
8. Analytical Synthesis: What Khaṇḍa 1 Reveals 
8.1 Social Architecture 
Khaṇḍa 1 reveals Maithil Brahmin society as organised along three 
simultaneous axes: gotra (patrilineal descent classification), mūla-grāma 
(home-cluster for residence and marriage eligibility), and mātṛkā (maternal 
clan for the dau-network). The interaction of these three axes creates a matrix 
of identity far more complex than simple patrilineal reckoning. The Pañjī is 
the instrument that maps this three-dimensional social space. 
8.2 Intellectual Geography 
The concentration of Mahāmahopādhyāya titles in the Khauaal–Sarisa–
Khandabla triangle, with secondary clusters at Kujoli and Darihara, 
corresponds precisely to the known centres of Navya-Nyāya philosophical 
activity in Mithilā. Gaṅgeśa Upādhyāya's home village of Kariān (Kardaha) 
lies within this zone. Khaṇḍa 1 thus provides the genealogical scaffolding for 
the social production of Navya-Nyāya scholarship — showing that 
intellectual lineages and biological lineages were actively maintained as 
parallel systems. 
8.3 Royal-Brahmin Symbiosis 
The Kādya/Kāśyapa gotra's special position — blessed with a panegyric 
verse and assigned the largest cluster of villages — reflects the historical 
alliance 
between 
the 
Karnāṭa 
dynasty 
(Nānyadeva, 
Gaṅgadeva, 
Narasiṃhadeva) and specific Brahmin families. The state-official titles 
embedded in genealogical entries (Śāntivigrāhika, Mahāmattaka, etc.) 
demonstrate that these were not merely honorary but functional positions 
passed within identified family lines. 
8.4 The Digitisation Significance 
The bilingual format of this volume — Mithilakshara alongside Kaithi-
transliterated Devanagari — represents the Pañjī Project's most ambitious 
intervention: rendering a script that fewer than 100, people can read into the 
Devanagari standard accessible to all Hindi-Maithili literates. Khaṇḍa 1's 65 
pages encode approximately 1, years of social history for roughly 5, named 
individuals across 122 villages. As a standalone data unit within the larger

document in the Videha digital archive.

10. Concluding Note 
Khaṇḍa 1 of the Prācīn Pañjī — the 'ancient genealogy' — is not ancient in 
the sense of being composed in antiquity. It is ancient in the sense that it 
records the deepest stratum of genealogical memory maintained by the 
Panjikar tradition: families and villages that were already established when 
the Pañjī system was instituted under the Karnāṭa kings in the 12th century

CE. The 65 pages analysed here constitute a living map of Maithil social 
history: 122 villages, 17 gotras, 55+ genealogical cycle-completions, 47 
documented Mahāmahopādhyāya scholars, and a unique administrative 
record of the Karnāṭa court hierarchy — all encoded in the compact, precise 
grammar of the suta/dau/mātṛkā notation system that the Panjikar tradition 
has maintained without interruption for over eight hundred years. 
The Videha digitisation project, by rendering this bilingual document into 
searchable Devanagari, transforms Khaṇḍa 1 from a manuscript held by a 
shrinking community of Panjikar practitioners into an open scholarly 
resource — completing the transition from milieu de mémoire (living 
memory) to lieu de mémoire (site of recorded memory) in the most 
productive sense of that theoretical distinction.

1. Overview and Structure of Pages 66–347 
Unlike the opening Khaṇḍa 1 (pages 1–65) which established the 
foundational 
genealogical

continuation 
section is 
characterised by far greater density and complexity: longer individual 
genealogical chains, more compound scholarly titles, a marked expansion 
into Nepal-adjacent geography (particularly around the Simaravāḍa–Rataulī 
corridor), and crucially, the insertion of dated marriage colophons — real-
time records embedded within the ancient genealogical frame — that bridge 
the 12th century Pañjī tradition with entries dated 01..2003, 09..2003, 
15..2003, 20..2004, 21..2004, and others. This fusion of deep time and 
contemporary event is unique in South Asian manuscript traditions.

2. Geographical Coverage: New Villages and Localities 
2.1 Dominant New Village Cluster: Karmaharā 
The single most significant geographical finding in pages 66–347 is the 
emergence of Karmaharā (karamahā) as the dominant reference village — 
appearing 292 times, surpassing even Māṇḍar's 174+ references in Khaṇḍa 
1's first 65 pages. Karmaharā functions as the primary maternal-line anchor 
for dozens of genealogical cycles in this section. Its density confirms that the 
Pañjī's geographical emphasis shifts eastward as the corpus progresses, with 
Karmaharā (identified with present-day Karmahe, Madhubani district) 
emerging as the central node of a dense web of scholarly and landowning 
families in the Madhubani–Sitamarhi corridor.

2.3 The Rataulī–Darihara Sub-Cluster 
A significant new geographic sub-system emerges in pages 66–347 around 
the Rataulī–Darihara corridor, distinct from the Darihara references in 
Khaṇḍa 1's first 65 pages. Rataulī in this section appears specifically as 
'Rataulī-Darihara' (rataulī dariharā sa~), marking it as a jointly-identified 
paired node — suggesting a hamlet administratively or genealogically 
attached to Darihara proper but distinct enough to require separate 
identification. The Rataulī families connect most frequently through 
Simaravāḍa (near Janakpur, Nepal), indicating a genealogical corridor that 
straddles the present-day India–Nepal border. 
The Bhauālavāsī entry (p.., §83) is especially remarkable: 'vāvūsutā 
Bhauālavāsī Amarāvatī-darihara-sāṃ Rāṃka dau' — 'Bābu's sons include 
[one who is] a Bhauāla-resident [Kathmandu Valley], [others at] Amarāvatī 
[and] Darihara via Rāṃka.' This is a direct record of Mithilā Brahmin 
diaspora reaching the Kathmandu Valley, linking Bhauāl (modern Nepal's 
former kingdom around Kathmandu) with Darihara in a single genealogical 
entry. 
3. Families and Lineage Chains: What Is Unique 
3.1 The Avalaṃba-Sarasvatī Title: A Unique Scholarly Honorific 
Page 1., section §79, preserves one of the most unusual compound titles in 
the entire Pañjī corpus: 'Mahāmahopādhyāya Hariśvara suto Avalaṃba-

Bateśa 
Puruṣottamo'

[i.e. specially designated] Mahāmahopādhyāya Bateśa Puruṣottama.' The 
compound 
'Avalaṃba-Sarasvatī-dāṅkit' 
means 
literally 
'bearing 
the

scholar was recognized as a support-pillar (avalaṃba = prop, support) of the 
goddess of learning. This title is found nowhere else in Khaṇḍa 1 and

The Bateśa Puruṣottama line then produces his son Sarasvatī Rāma — whose 
very personal name echoes his father's title — who continues to Karmaharā 
(the dominant village of this section), confirming that the most rarified 
scholarly honors cluster around the Karmaharā–Darihara–Khauāla triangle. 
3.2 The Mahārāja Mādhava Siṃha Royal Entry 
Pages 1. and 1.127–1. preserve one of the most extraordinary records in the 
entire Pañjī corpus: the direct genealogical placement of the Mithilā royal 
family of Mādhavasiṃha within the Brahmin genealogical register. This is 
not merely honorific — it follows the full suta/dau notation grammar and 
places Mādhavasiṃha as a factual dau (maternal nephew's descendant) 
within a specified lineage chain.

The Sarvajña Garība Entry 
The epithet 'Sarvajña Garība' — literally 'All-Knowing Pauper' — is one of 
the most striking personal designations in the corpus. Appearing at p.. as a 
dau-source ('Pāli saṃ Sarvajñāna Garība dau'), the name encodes a paradox 
familiar in Indian philosophical traditions: the scholar who possesses 
omniscient knowledge but renounces all material wealth. The name itself has 
been absorbed into the genealogical system as a family identifier, suggesting 
that an ancestor's spiritual achievement was so distinctive that it became the 
family's genealogical calling-card for subsequent generations. 
3.4 The Cāturmāsika Mansabdār Hazārī Entry 
Page 1., section §127, preserves a remarkable title-cluster that signals the 
encounter between the Pañjī's Brahmin genealogical system and the Mughal

Caudhary Māniknandana.' The 'Mansabdār Hazārī' (commander of 1000 
troops in the Mughal mansab system) is integrated seamlessly into the Pañjī's 
suta-chain notation, demonstrating that by the time this entry was recorded, 
Mughal administrative titles had been absorbed into Mithilā's genealogical 
legitimation apparatus. The addition of 'Cāturmāsika' (four-monthly, possibly 
indicating seasonal administrative appointment or ceremonial frequency) 
further refines the entry's administrative-historical value. 
This family's genealogy continues to produce 'Rāja Dulārasiṃha Bābu 
Ekalāla Siṃha alias Tejānanda Siṃha' and eventually 'Mahārāja Vedānanda 
Siṃha' whose son 'Kalikarnāvatāra Rāja Līlānanda Siṃha Bahādur' receives 
the Khalsa-era Rajput title 'Bahādur' — tracing the family's trajectory from 
Mughal mansabdār through late-Mughal to Rajput-styled local kingship 
within a single genealogical chain spanning perhaps six to eight generations. 
3.5 The Mādhavasiṃha Royal Sub-Cluster: Sons Recorded 
One of the most important genealogical records in pages 66–347 is the 
complete listing of Mādhavasiṃha's sons (p.., §115–116). Section §115 
records: 'Vivdhityādi Mahārāja Mādhavasiṃha suto Bābu Ramāpati Siṃha' 
followed by a complete list of sons with their sibling-rank notation codes, 
unusual for royal entries which normally appear only in summary form in the 
Pañjī. 
Section §116 then records a further royal lineage culminating with 
'Mithilābhūṣaṇa Mahārāja Sundar suto Mithilānātha Mithileśvara Mahārāja 
Narapati' — placing both the 'Sundar' branch and the 'Mādhavasiṃha' branch 
as distinct sons of a common ancestor in the Khaṇḍabalā dau-network. The 
Mithilā royal family is thus placed within the Brahmin Pañjī as legitimate 
members of the dau-bīji network, their kingship validated by genealogical 
integration with the very families that provided them religious legitimacy. 
3.6 The Bhramar Mātṛka-Cakra and Its Scholar Chain 
Page 1., §36 records the completion of the 'Bhramar mātṛka-cakra' — a cycle 
named after the ancestor Bhramar. What makes this cycle unique is that it is 
explicitly linked to the Belauñca village entry for a Mahāmahopādhyāya line, 
and the Bhramar figure is identified as having been the ancestor of the

a documented three-generation chain linking a named ancestor (Bhramar) to 
a named scholarly title (Jyotirvimay) to a named village (Belauñca) — the 
kind of precision that makes the Pañjī an unparalleled historical source.

5. Marriage Colophons: Dated Contemporary Records 
One of the most remarkable features of pages 66–347 — unique in the Pañjī 
corpus as far as can be determined — is the insertion of dated marriage 
records (vivāha-prakaraṇa) within the genealogical chains. These colophons, 
embedded mid-text, record specific marriages with calendrical dates 
expressed in both traditional Pañcāṅga notation (tithi, vara, pakṣa, māsa) and 
Common Era dates (sometimes with day names in English or numerical 
DD.MM.YYYY format). They transform a historical manuscript into a living 
record that bridges the 12th century origin of the Pañjī tradition to the year 
2004 CE.

The use of English day-names ('Tuesday', 'Monday Morning') alongside 
Sanskrit tithi notation in documents dated 2003–2004 is linguistically 
extraordinary. It reflects the Panjikar's pragmatic adaptation: the traditional 
calendar (tithi-based) remained the formal genealogical record, but English 
day-names were added as a concession to the solar-calendar literacy of the 
families being registered. This is the Pañjī tradition not fossilising but 
actively evolving in real time.

The closing formula for the Mahāmahopādhyāya Bhagīratha colophon reads: 
'Ete Mahāmahopādhyāya Bhagīratha-prasiddha Medhā-santati samāptāḥ | 
Māgha Śukla Trayodaśyāṃ Kuje || 03..2004' — 'The lineage of the renowned 
Mahāmahopādhyāya Bhagīratha [thus] ends. [Dated:] Māgha Bright 
Thirteenth, Tuesday, 03..2004.' This is both a genealogical closure and a 
dated manuscript colophon, giving the exact date of the Panjikar's work — 
February 3, 2004. 
6. Notable Individual Entries 
6.1 The 'Kavi Vidyāpati' Entry 
Page 1., section §87, preserves a late but explicit entry: 'devanātha suto Kavi 
Vidyāpati Cahī sodarapura saṃ jagannātha dau' — 'Devanātha's son: the poet 
Vidyāpati [placed at] Cahī via Sodarapura through Jagannātha.' This is 
distinct from the Khaṇḍa 1 §4 placement in pages 1–65 and represents a 
second Pañjī placement of Vidyāpati within the genealogical matrix, this 
time at a different village (Cahī) and with a different dau-chain, suggesting 
either that the Panjikar maintained two parallel records for the poet, or that 
two members of the same family carried the name. 
6.2 The Ṭhākura Prefix Families 
A distinctive feature of pages 66–347 is the proliferation of 'Ṭhākura' (ṭha. / 
ṭhao) prefixed individuals — a designation for persons of high Kṣatriya or 
upper-Brahmin status. In this section, 'Ṭhākura' is used not merely as a 
respectful title but as a formal genealogical prefix indicating that the 
individual concerned has a confirmed marriage-record (vivāha-prakaraṇa) 
appended to his entry. The Ṭhākura-prefix persons whose marriages are 
recorded constitute a distinct sub-register within the Pañjī — a marriage 
registry embedded in the genealogical text — with each Ṭhākura entry 
concluded by the formula 'evaṃ Ṭh. X vivāha:' followed by a date. 
6.3 The Pāṃ Prefix — Scholar-Family Marker 
The prefix 'Pāṃ' (pā~) or 'Pāñca' appears consistently before names of 
individuals from specific scholarly families in pages 66–347. Examples: 
'Pāṃ Ratidhara', 'Pāṃ Keśava', 'Pāṃ Ramadeva', 'Pāṃ Hariśvara'. This prefix 
— short for Pañcāyatī or perhaps Pañca-śrotriya — indicates that the family 
holds the highest Brahmin ranking (the 'Pañca' or five-fold śrotriya

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 229 
classification) within the Mithilā caste hierarchy. These are the families from 
whom Mahāmahopādhyāya title-holders most frequently emerge. 
6.4 The Bhauālavāsī — Nepal Diaspora 
As noted in Section 2, the Bhauālavāsī entry (p..) is the single most 
geographically distant reference in the entire Pañjī. 'Bhauāl' (bhauāla) 
identifies the Bagmati corridor of present-day Nepal — the valley region 
around Kathmandu. The entry documents a son of the Bābu family who had 
settled in this region, while his brothers are placed at Amarāvatī (a locality 
name) and Darihara. The Panjikar's ability to track this diaspora and 
incorporate it into the official genealogical register demonstrates the 
remarkable organisational reach of the Pañjī system. 
6.5 The Vaidika Sub-Lineage 
A substantial number of individuals in pages 66–347 bear the prefix 'Vaidika' 
(vaidika), 
indicating 
Vedic-ritual 
specialist 
status. 
Unlike 
'Mahāmahopādhyāya' which denotes philosophical-scholarly excellence, 
'Vaidika' marks families whose primary hereditary function was the 
performance of Vedic rituals. Pages 1.119–1. contain a particularly dense 
cluster: 'Vaidika Yudanātha', 'Vaidika Viśvambhara', 'Vaidika Jīvanātha', 
'Vaidika Vanmālī', 'Vaidika Sarvamjñāna'. The Vaidika families form a 
parallel sub-system to the Naiyāyika (logic-philosophy) families, both 
operating within the same genealogical matrix but traceable through different 
title traditions. 
6.6 Rāya and Rāyata Families 
The title 'Rāya' (rāya) — a revenue-officer designation deriving from Persian 
'Rāy' (regional officer/accountant) — appears frequently as a prefix in pages 
66–347, particularly in contexts linking Brahmin families to Mughal and 
post-Mughal administrative service. Notable examples include: 'Rāya Uttama 
Nārāyaṇa', 'Rāya Śaṃkara', 'Rāya Hemānagada', 'Rāya Naya', 'Rāya Candra 
Nārāyaṇa'. These families occupy an intermediate position in the Mithilā 
social hierarchy — between the purely scholarly Mahāmahopādhyāya 
families and the ruling Kṣatriya lineages — and their incorporation into the 
Pañjī confirms that the register tracked administrative-economic status 
alongside ritual-scholarly credentials.

8. Synthesis: What Pages 66–347 Add to the Scholarly 
Record 
8.1 The Temporal Collapse 
The most intellectually significant feature of pages 66–347 is what may be 
called the 'temporal collapse' — the point at which an ancient genealogical 
register, rooted in the 12th century, suddenly admits dated entries from 2003 
and 2004. This is not merely a curiosity: it is documentary evidence that the 
Pañjī tradition remained not only alive but juridically active until at least 
2004. The marriage colophons embedded in this section were presumably 
inserted by the same Panjikar who was collating the ancient chains, meaning 
that the Panjikar's office was still being consulted for matrimonial validation 
in the 21st century — a living institutional continuity of over 800 years. 
8.2 Karmaharā as Analytical Key 
The emergence of Karmaharā as the highest-frequency reference village in 
this section — appearing 292 times against Māṇḍar's 174 in Khaṇḍa 1 pages 
1–65 — is not merely a quantitative finding. It reflects a genuine historical 
shift in Mithilā's scholarly-familial geography: by the period that this section 
of the Pañjī was crystallising (probably 17th–18th century for the core 
chains, with 21st-century additions), Karmaharā had become the dominant 
Brahmin intellectual centre of the eastern Tirhut region, perhaps at the 
expense of the older Māṇḍar–Sarisa–Khauāla triangle documented in the first 
section. 
8.3 The Royal Integration 
The detailed recording of Mādhavasiṃha's sons and the Mithilārāja 
genealogy within a Brahmin genealogical register represents the Pañjī at its 
most politically significant. By placing the ruling family within the dau-bīji 
matrix, the Panjikar was performing an act of social constitution: declaring 
that the king's legitimacy was not merely military or hereditary but 
genealogically certified by the same system that certified every Brahmin 
family's social status. This was the Pañjī's deepest political function — and 
pages 66–347 preserve it in its most explicit surviving form.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 232 
8.4 The Nepal Corridor 
The three Nepal-linked entries in pages 66–347 — Bhauālavāsī (Kathmandu 
Valley diaspora), Simaravāḍ (Janakpur corridor), and Maliṅgiyā Kujaulī 
(Malingiya near Nepal border) — confirm what historical scholarship has 
long suspected: the Mithilā Brahmin community was not bounded by 
political borders. The Pañjī's tracking of diaspora families into the 
Kathmandu Valley demonstrates an organisational reach that transcended the 
Gorakhā–Mithilā border long before that border was formalised. These 
entries are primary sources for the history of Brahmin migration from Tirhut 
into Nepal. 
8.5 The Script Continuity 
The bilingual format maintained throughout pages 66–347 — Mithilakshara 
alongside Kaithi-Devanagari — is itself a political statement. The 
Mithilakshara script, increasingly endangered, is here preserved alongside its 
transliteration, ensuring that the genealogical content could be read by both 
the traditional Panjikar community (Mithilakshara-literate) and the broader 
Devanagari-literate population. The Videha digitisation project, by encoding 
this section in searchable text, extends this transliteration project into the 
digital era, potentially making the Pañjī's records accessible to genealogical 
researchers worldwide for the first time.

10. Concluding Note 
Pages 66–347 of the Panji Prabandha of Mithila — covering Khaṇḍa 1's 
continuation from §58 onwards — constitute a qualitative advance over the 
foundational section in pages 1–65. Where the first section established the 
grammar and principal nodes of the genealogical system, this section 
demonstrates its full operational complexity: new villages entering the

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 234 
matrix, new scholarly titles being coined and recorded, royal families being 
incorporated, Nepal-linked diaspora being tracked, and — most astonishingly 
— real-time marriage events from 2003 and 2004 being inserted into an 800-
year-old genealogical tradition without interruption or rupture. 
The 292 appearances of Karmaharā alone justify the characterisation of this 
section as a distinct geographical epoch within the Pañjī: not the 12th-century 
Halāyudha-anchored world of Gaṅgolī and Māṇḍar, but the fully matured 
17th–18th century (and ongoing) world of the eastern Tirhut intellectual 
heartland, where the Karmaharā–Darihara–Khauāla triangle had become the 
new centre of Maithil scholarly gravity. 
The marriage colophons — 24 or more distinct records with CE dates — are 
without parallel in Indian genealogical manuscript traditions. They 
demonstrate that the Pañjī was not a historical archive but a juridical 
instrument: a living constitution of Maithil social order, consulted and 
updated in real time by families who continued to regard genealogical 
certification as the foundation of social legitimacy. The Videha digitisation 
project, by making this section available in searchable Devanagari, has 
transformed a closed institutional document into an open scholarly resource 
— completing a process that the Panjikar tradition began in Mithilakshara 
eight centuries ago.

Khaṇḍa 2: Pages 348–690 
A Comprehensive Research Report on Villages, Families, Lineages,

Research report prepared for Videha Parallel Literature Movement | May

1. Overview: Pages 348–690 in the Panji Corpus 
The PDF Final_348-690.pdf spans 160 pages of the Panji Prabandha of 
Mithila, covering document pages 348 through 690. This is the largest of the 
three volumes analysed, and it represents the fullest flowering of the Pañjī 
tradition — genealogical density at its maximum, geographical spread at its 
widest, and the encounter with Indian modernity at its most vivid. Where 
pages 1–65 established the 12th-century genealogical grammar and pages 
66–347 showed that grammar under expansion, pages 348–690 show it under 
adaptation: a system designed for pre-modern Brahmin society absorbing

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 236 
railways, universities, police hierarchies, income tax offices, inter-religious 
marriages in Sweden, and British imperial honours. 
The dominant geographical shift from previous volumes is striking: 
Sodarapura (sodara ura / sodara ura) emerges as the single highest-frequency 
village in this volume with 853 appearances, overtaking Karmaharā (580) 
and Māṇḍar (621). This signals a new geographic gravity centre — 
Sodarapura appears to function as the primary administrative node for the 
eastern Tirhut Brahmin population recorded in this section. Darihara (558), 
Khauāla (361), and Narauna (376) form the secondary cluster, with Hariakha 
(331), Khaṇḍabalā (326), and Valiyāsa (291) completing the dense inner 
network. 
The 160 pages contain 41 vivāha (marriage) records — significantly more 
per page than previous volumes — and 91 Mahārāja-titled entries, reflecting 
a royal lineage that reaches extraordinary complexity in the Mādhavasiṃha, 
Chhatrasiṃha, Rāmeśvarasiṃha, and Kāmeśvarasiṃha dynastic branches. 
The Mahāmahopādhyāya abbreviation (mao mao) appears 116 times, 
confirming sustained scholarly production even as modernising pressures 
reshape the vocabulary of achievement.

3. Notable Families and Lineage Chains 
3.1 The Royal Genealogical Complex: Mādhavasiṃha to 
Kāmeśvarasiṃha 
Pages 348–690 contain the most elaborate royal genealogy in the entire Pañjī 
corpus. The Mithilā royal family, first introduced in pages 66–347, here 
reaches its full documented complexity across multiple generations and 
multiple marriage records. The central figure is Mahārāja Mādhava Siṃha, 
whose descendants are tracked through four distinct marriage events 
(chaturtha vivāha = fourth marriage) and whose sons, grandsons, and great-
grandsons span military, administrative, and scholarly careers in both 
traditional and colonial frameworks.
